one_line_takeaway: A layered symbolic+linguistic text-steganography canary framework achieves 100% detection through benign handling and standard sanitization, and its hybrid Mode B (ZW+HG+LM) sustains 97% recovery even under targeted adversarial stripping (though not LLM paraphrasing), with zero false positives across 800 trials and successful end-to-end interception of a simulated LLM-orchestrated ransomware upload under Tier-0 transport.
key_finding: All four individual methods (M1-M4) achieve 100% recovery at Tier 0 and remain intact through Tier 1 (T01-T04: copy-paste, line reflow, smart quotes, trailing-whitespace strip). At Tier 2 the methods diverge: WS fails under NFKC normalization/whitespace collapse (0%), ZW fails under format-character stripping (0%), while HG and LM survive Tier 2 fully (100%). At Tier 3, targeted adversarial transforms destroy their intended target (ZW stripping kills ZW, homoglyph normalization kills HG, full ASCII strip kills all symbolic methods at 0%), but LM survives at 98% (attributed to a GPT-2 byte-level BPE vocabulary artifact producing occasional non-ASCII tokens). At Tier 4, an inverse pattern appears: punctuation/case stripping (T11) preserves WS/ZW (100%) but collapses HG to 8% and LM to 0%; LLM-based paraphrase (T12) destroys all methods (0%). For layered configurations: M5 (WS+ZW+HG, Mode A) matches its strongest single component (HG) exactly -- 100% through Tier-2/Tier-1+2, 0% at Tier-3 and beyond -- showing symbolic stacking adds redundancy without altering individual method behavior. M6 (ZW+HG+LM, Mode B/recommended) is the only configuration that survives adversarial non-semantic processing: 99% at Tier-1, 99% at Tier-2/Tier-1+2, and 97% recovery at Tier-3 and Tier-1+2+3 (driven by the LM layer). M7 (adds WS to the hybrid stack) demonstrates genuine cross-layer interference: the LM layer collapses to 0% at Tier-3 in isolation (because WS's Unicode-space substitutions, when stripped by full-ASCII-strip, delete bytes the linguistic decoder needs for token alignment -- unlike ZW/HG which are byte-exact invertible), yet recovers 96% under the cumulative Tier-1+2+3 chain because an earlier NFKC normalization step (T05) converts the WS spaces back to ASCII before the destructive T10 step runs, confirming a transform-order dependency. All 4 tiers = 0 verified false positives across 800 trials (4 decoders x 100 unmarked documents x 2 verification schemes: HMAC against a 10^7-token registry, P_fp <= 2.9x10^-32; EdDSA against 10^3 public keys, P_fp <= 2.9x10^-36) -- though the LM decoder always returns a non-None candidate (200/200), all such candidates fail cryptographic verification. Symbolic methods run sub-millisecond on CPU (e.g., M5 HMAC: 0.706ms encode / 0.536ms decode); the linguistic method is GPU-bound and dominates cost, scaling with payload size (M4 HMAC: ~1.15s encode/0.17s decode; M4 EdDSA: ~3.58s encode/0.51s decode, with EdDSA's 3.9x larger payload also lowering decode success to 82% vs. 99% under HMAC due to the same GPT-2 vocabulary artifact at greater scale). Deployment-scale estimate: Mode A marks 500 files in under 0.5s; Mode B requires ~12 minutes (HMAC) to ~33 minutes (EdDSA) of GPU time for 500 files, framed as a one-time batch cost. In the end-to-end ransomware case study (Tier-0 conditions, HMAC scheme, 288-file simulated share with 2 canary files at ~0.7% density), the reverse-proxy detection pipeline successfully intercepted and blocked the ransomware's canary-bearing upload during the reconnaissance phase, before file encryption began.
core_contribution: The paper (Raz, Putrevu, Udeshi, Krishnamurthy, Khorrami, Karri; NYU) presents a defensive framework for detecting unauthorized submission of controlled documents to LLM services by embedding cryptographically-derived identifiers into plaintext 'canary' documents using layered text steganography. Two deployment modes are proposed and implemented: Mode A marks existing sensitive documents with a stacked symbolic encoding (whitespace substitution WS, zero-width character insertion ZW, homoglyph substitution HG applied on disjoint Unicode surfaces); Mode B generates synthetic canary documents via linguistic steganography (Self-Adjusting Arithmetic Coding, SAAC, over GPT-2 124M) augmented with compatible symbolic layers (ZW + HG, WS excluded due to an invertibility constraint). Identifiers are constructed via HMAC-SHA256 (shared-key, 18-byte framed payload) or Ed25519/EdDSA (public-key, 70-byte framed payload). A vendor-side (or proxy-side) pre-ingestion filter extracts and verifies identifiers before LLM processing, without semantic classification of content. The paper's own contributions (explicitly stated as C1-C3) are: (C1) the layered symbolic+linguistic canary framework itself with dual verification schemes; (C2) a four-tier transport-transform taxonomy (Tier 1 benign/incidental, Tier 2 standard sanitization, Tier 3 targeted adversarial, Tier 4 special/semantic) with 12 individual transforms and 6 composite chains, used to systematically evaluate 7 method configurations (M1-M7); (C3) an end-to-end validation against a PromptLock/Ransomware-3.0-style LLM-orchestrated ransomware pipeline in a sandboxed environment, detecting and blocking canary-bearing uploads during reconnaissance before encryption.
threat_model: Adversary has already obtained sensitive files (via compromise, lateral movement, insider access, or supply-chain abuse) and must submit them to an external, cloud-hosted LLM for analysis (reconnaissance, credential/secret extraction, summarization, or payload generation) to weaponize them. Two motivating pathways are modeled: (1) AI-powered malware/agentic adversaries (e.g., LLM-orchestrated ransomware such as PromptLock, AI-assisted espionage triage, automated credential harvesters, social-engineering tools) that depend on an external LLM as an analysis engine; (2) incidental/insider uploads by authorized users pasting sensitive content into third-party AI services. The adversary controls content transport/pre-processing before submission but does not possess the organization's secret key or exact embedding configuration; it may know the general defense class but not method selection, parameters, or key material. Explicit scope exclusions: local/offline LLMs (bypass the vendor-side ingestion boundary entirely), binary/multimedia steganography, file-format watermarks (PDF metadata/OOXML fields) as primary signal, network-level exfiltration detection, automated large-scale canary-content generation, and the legal/privacy framework for vendor cooperation. The defense assumes a cooperating AI vendor or enterprise-controlled monitoring proxy runs extraction/verification on inbound plaintext prior to model processing; key distribution and notification protocols are explicitly out of scope.
limitations: The authors' own scope restrictions and observed weaknesses (from the text made available): (1) Local/offline LLMs bypass the entire defense since detection depends on a cooperating vendor-side or proxy ingestion boundary -- explicitly acknowledged as an accepted scope restriction rather than a solved case. (2) Tier 4 transforms (punctuation/case stripping, and especially LLM-based semantic paraphrase) defeat essentially all methods (paraphrase: 0% across every configuration), and the paper explicitly frames Tier 4 as an 'upper-bound adversarial capability evaluated for completeness rather than robustness' -- i.e., the framework does not claim resilience against a paraphrasing adversary. (3) The linguistic method's failures are tied to an idiosyncratic GPT-2 124M byte-level BPE vocabulary artifact (occasional non-ASCII token emission causing arithmetic-coding desynchronization on strip), responsible for the 2% Tier-3 loss, the 4-5%/1-5% encode failures in stacked configs, and the pronounced EdDSA decode-success drop to 82%; the authors argue a larger/cleaner-vocabulary model would reduce this but this is not empirically demonstrated in the paper. (4) Adding WS to the hybrid stack (M7) introduces real cross-layer interference (LM collapses to 0% at Tier-3) that depends on transform ordering, illustrating that composition is not universally safe and requires the invertibility discipline the authors identify (WS is excluded from the recommended Mode B for exactly this reason). (5) The end-to-end case study validates detection only under Tier-0 (raw, unmodified) transport conditions -- it does not test the ransomware pipeline against Tier 1-4 transforms, so the demonstrated block is not evidence the defense would hold if the malware itself applied sanitizing or paraphrasing transforms before upload. (6) Canary density in the case study is very low (~0.7%, 2 of 288 files), and the paper does not report false-negative behavior at that density; only a favorable case is shown. (7) Homoglyph substitution's capacity and coverage depend on the presence of eligible Latin characters (35-40% of typical prose) and only 18 curated confusable pairs are used, and it is vulnerable to Unicode-security-aware steganalysis, per the paper's own discussion in the background section.

datasets_used: 100 English prose documents sampled from Wikipedia via the HuggingFace datasets library, filtered to a minimum of 3,000 characters and truncated to that target at word boundaries (~3KB documents)
Simulated organizational file share of 288 files (10 pseudo-sensitive, 2 canary candidates) inside a QEMU/KVM Ubuntu 20.04 VM for the end-to-end ransomware case study
quantitative_results: M1-M4 (WS, ZW, HG, LM individually): 100% recovery at Tier 0 and Tier 1
Tier 2: WS 0%, ZW 0% (fails on its own targeted transform: NFKC/whitespace-collapse for WS, format-char strip for ZW), HG 100%, LM 100%
Tier 4 (T11 punct/case strip): WS 100%, ZW 100%, HG 8%, LM 0%; (T12 LLM paraphrase): all methods 0%
M5 (WS+ZW+HG, Mode A, ANY): 100% Tier-1, 100% Tier-2, 0% Tier-3, 100% Tier-1+2, 0% Tier-1+2+3, 0% Tier-4
M6 (ZW+HG+LM, Mode B, ANY): 99% Tier-1, 99% Tier-2, 97% Tier-3, 99% Tier-1+2, 97% Tier-1+2+3, 0% Tier-4
M7 (WS+ZW+HG+LM, ANY): 98% Tier-1, 98% Tier-2, 0% Tier-3, 98% Tier-1+2, 96% Tier-1+2+3, 0% Tier-4 (illustrating cross-layer interference and order dependency)
Stack compatibility (M7, Tier-0, 100 files): control LM decode 100/100; after full 4-layer encode, all layers and byte-exact restoration succeed on 98/100 (2% loss from GPT-2 vocabulary artifact)

False positives: 0/400 HMAC decodes and 0/400 EdDSA decodes across 800 total trials (4 decoders x 100 unmarked docs x 2 schemes); LM always returns a non-None candidate (200/200) but 200/200 rejected on verification
HMAC false-positive bound: P_fp <= 10^7 x 2^-128 ~= 2.9x10^-32 (10^3 orgs x 10^4 file IDs registry)
EdDSA false-positive bound: P_fp <= 10^3 x 2^-128 ~= 2.9x10^-36 (10^3 registered public keys)
Latency, HMAC scheme: M1(WS) 0.149ms enc/0.172ms dec; M5(WS+ZW+HG) 0.706ms enc/0.536ms dec; M4(LM) 1,151ms enc/172ms dec (GPU); M6(ZW+HG+LM) 1,403ms enc/194ms dec, 96% enc/dec success
Latency, EdDSA scheme: M5(WS+ZW+HG) 0.898ms enc/0.693ms dec; M4(LM) 3,584ms enc/512ms dec, 99% enc/82% dec success; M6 3,959ms enc/508ms dec, 95% enc/95% dec success
Deployment scale: Mode A (M5) marks 500 files in under 0.5s; Mode B (M6) requires ~12 minutes (HMAC) to ~33 minutes (EdDSA) of GPU time for 500 files

End-to-end case study: 288-file simulated share, 10 pseudo-sensitive files, 2 canary files (~0.7% density); reverse-proxy pipeline detected and blocked the canary-bearing upload during the ransomware's reconnaissance phase under Tier-0 (raw) transport, before encryption began
baselines_compared: No external prior-work baseline systems were run head-to-head; comparison to prior defenses (endpoint/network DLP, active honeytoken canaries with network callbacks, document watermarking) is qualitative (Table 1: channel-agnostic, survives copy-paste, passive/no-callback, format-agnostic, works offline/Tor), not an empirical benchmark
Internal baselines: M1-M4 (single-method WS/ZW/HG/LM) serve as ablation baselines against which the stacked configurations M5-M7 are compared

SAAC linguistic steganography baseline is adopted from prior work (Ziegler et al. GPT-2 arithmetic coding steganography, and Self-Adjusting Arithmetic Coding) rather than a novel encoding scheme -- the paper's contribution here is the composition of SAAC with symbolic layers, not the underlying linguistic stego algorithm itself
